question: Which Cursor surface is most associated with accelerating local edits at the cursor position?
options: Inline completion (autocomplete in the editor).
Background agents running on a server.
The trust center landing page.
The pricing calculator.
explanation: Inline completion is the surface that compresses local edits — the smallest, fastest acceleration story. Bigger surfaces (chat, composer, agents) tackle larger workflows.

question: Which surface is most appropriate for ‘exploration, clarification, and guided changes’ inside the IDE?
options: Inline completion.
Chat grounded in the repo.
The procurement portal.
The status page.
explanation: Chat is the conversational surface for exploration and guided changes. It is where developers ask ‘where does this live?’ and ‘what would this refactor look like?’

question: Composer / agent-style flows in Cursor are most useful for:
options: Single-character typo fixes.
Multi-step changes across files where governance and validation matter most.
Replacing the company SSO provider.
Generating finance dashboards.
explanation: Composer-style flows shine on multi-step, multi-file changes — exactly where you also need strong validation and review, which is why governance comes up.

question: Which is the most credible way to position background agents to a security-conscious buyer?
options: ‘They run unsupervised, so you don’t need any guardrails.’
‘They reduce toil when teams have clear guardrails — what controls would you require to trust them on real work?’
‘Background agents are not real, just a marketing term.’
‘They eliminate the need for code review entirely.’
explanation: Lead with value-with-guardrails and turn the question back to their required controls. That posture demonstrates respect for governance and earns the conversation.

question: Which acronym does Security/IT typically reference when asking about identity provider integration?
options: SSO / SAML.
JPEG / GIF.
TCP / UDP.
RAM / ROM.
explanation: SSO via SAML (or OIDC) is the standard identity ask. Confirm support and configuration specifics from primary trust documentation rather than hand-waving.

question: What does SCIM most directly enable for an enterprise rolling out Cursor?
options: Automated user lifecycle: provisioning, group membership, and deprovisioning from the directory.
Renaming PRs in bulk.
Faster CPU performance on developer laptops.
Replacing the team’s git host.
explanation: SCIM is the lifecycle story: when an employee joins, changes role, or leaves, their access changes automatically — required at enterprise scale and audited by IT.

question: When a buyer asks about ‘model access controls,’ they most likely want to know:
options: Whether they can constrain which models are available to which teams or for which data.
Whether the model has a pet name.
Whether the model can be used after business hours.
Whether the model writes poetry.
explanation: Enterprises need policy gates on model usage — by team, environment, or data sensitivity. That is the conversation, not benchmark scores.

question: The most credible thing to do when you are unsure of a Cursor security detail in front of a CISO is:
options: Make up a confident-sounding answer to keep momentum.
Acknowledge the uncertainty, point to primary trust/security documentation, and follow up with the specialist.
Change the topic to pricing.
Promise the feature exists in next quarter’s release.
explanation: Strategic credibility is built on precise sourcing. CISOs will respect ‘let me confirm against the trust docs and follow up’ far more than improvisation.

question: Public marketing stats about Cursor are best used as:
options: Contractual commitments quoted verbatim in the MSA.
Hypotheses to validate against the buyer’s context, workflows, and evaluation criteria.
Proof that further discovery is unnecessary.
Replacements for any internal benchmarks.
explanation: Treat marketing stats as starting hypotheses and prove them in-account. Quoting them as contractual facts is the fastest way to lose CISO trust.

question: Which statement about ‘developer preference’ is most credible in an enterprise evaluation?
options: It is irrelevant because Security makes the call.
It only matters for startups under twenty engineers.
It matters and should be measured the way the org actually evaluates — not as a generic NPS score.
It can be guaranteed by the vendor in writing.
explanation: Developer preference is real signal but each org measures it differently — surveys, opt-in rates, evaluation rubrics. Define ‘preference’ in their language, then measure it.

question: ‘MCP controls’ in an enterprise context refer to:
options: Controlling which integrations are allowed to be wired up via MCP.
Controlling the IDE’s color theme.
Controlling which programming languages developers can use.
Controlling office WiFi.
explanation: When buyers say ‘MCP controls,’ they mean policy over which integrations engineers can actually connect — and how that surface is observable.

question: Which workflow is a strong fit to attach to Cursor’s composer/agent-style story?
options: Onboarding new engineers into a large unfamiliar codebase.
Choosing a corporate health insurance plan.
Hosting a launch party for the marketing team.
Designing a new physical office floor plan.
explanation: Onboarding into a big repo is exactly the kind of multi-file, context-heavy workflow Cursor’s richer surfaces are designed to compress.

question: What does ‘Privacy Mode’ generally refer to in dev-tool sales conversations?
options: A posture about whether code or prompts are stored or used to train models — described accurately, not hand-waved.
A feature that hides the IDE icon from the dock.
A discount tier for nonprofits.
A keyboard shortcut to clear the chat.
explanation: Privacy Mode is a data-handling posture. Describe its scope precisely and confirm against the trust center — buyers will ask how it applies to code, prompts, and tool calls.

question: ‘Zero Data Retention’ (where applicable) most directly addresses which buyer concern?
options: Whether prompts and outputs are stored, and for how long, in the assistant’s pipeline.
How fast the IDE loads on cold start.
Whether the assistant is offered as open source.
Whether the company offers free t-shirts at conferences.
explanation: ZDR speaks to retention. Always confirm packaging and the exact scope on Cursor’s trust materials before quoting it as a contractual fact.

question: Which behavior most damages a strategic seller’s credibility on Cursor enterprise controls?
options: Citing the trust center as the source of record.
Distinguishing between current packaging and roadmap.
Quoting marketing language as if it were a contractual commitment.
Inviting a Cursor security specialist into the conversation.
explanation: Treating marketing copy as contract is the classic credibility burner. Sourcing, scoping, and inviting specialists are credibility builders.

question: Which question best converts a Cursor ‘merge frequency’ proof point into discovery?
options: ‘Where is your team trying to increase merge frequency, and what is currently in the way?’
‘Do you like the color of our website?’
‘How many engineers can you fire?’
‘Will you sign today?’
explanation: The pattern is: take the marketing stat, turn it into a question that surfaces the buyer’s reality, and learn whether their context aligns.

question: ‘Composer / agent-style flows’ require strong governance because they:
options: Coordinate multi-step changes across many files where mistakes propagate quickly.
Require new physical hardware on every laptop.
Cannot be reverted with git.
Disable the IDE’s syntax highlighting.
explanation: Higher autonomy means changes can ripple. That is why review, testing, and policy gates matter most exactly where the leverage is highest.

question: When orienting a buyer to Cursor in roughly one minute, the most useful frame is:
options: A walkthrough of every UI tab.
A list of Cursor’s competitors.
Surfaces (inline completion, chat, composer/agent, background) → which workflow each accelerates.
A reading of the Terms of Service.
explanation: Surface → workflow mapping is the durable mental model. It connects features to outcomes the buyer cares about, in the time the buyer actually has.

question: Which is a credible enterprise ‘proof point as hypothesis’ statement?
options: ‘Some teams report meaningfully shorter review cycles — would you like to design a small evaluation in your repo to see whether the same shape holds for you?’
‘We guarantee a 10x productivity boost for every engineer in writing.’
‘You don’t need to evaluate — just sign.’
‘Trust me, it works.’
explanation: Frame proof as a hypothesis the buyer co-validates. That respects the engineering culture and produces evidence that survives scrutiny.

question: Which is NOT a Cursor surface that sellers should be able to orient a buyer to?
options: Inline completion.
Chat.
Composer / agent-style flows.
Cloud GPU rental marketplace.
explanation: Cursor’s seller-relevant surfaces are inline completion, chat, composer/agent flows, and adjacent automation. A GPU rental marketplace is not part of the product story.

question: ‘Adjacent automation workflows’ in Cursor’s story tend to deliver value most when:
options: Engineers want a tool to act with no constraints.
Teams have clear guardrails and a known repeatable workflow worth automating.
There are no tests in the repo.
Code is written entirely by hand only.
explanation: Toil-reducing automation lands when the workflow is repeatable and the guardrails are explicit. That is also how it stays trusted at scale.

question: Which question is most useful to surface review-bottleneck pain in a Cursor discovery?
options: ‘How long does the average PR sit waiting for review, and what is the cost when it stalls?’
‘How many monitors does each engineer have?’
‘What font size do reviewers prefer?’
‘What time zone is your CFO in?’
explanation: Tie review latency to a measurable cost. That converts a vague pain into a number worth fixing — and into a workflow Cursor can credibly accelerate.

question: ‘Directory integration’ usually refers to:
options: Linking the assistant with the enterprise identity directory (e.g. Okta, Azure AD) so accounts and groups stay in sync.
Indexing all files in a single git directory.
Choosing which IDE folder shows on the left.
Automatically renaming all directories in the repo.
explanation: Identity directory integration is the lifecycle backbone. SSO authenticates; SCIM provisions; the directory is the source of truth.

question: Which behavior signals strategic-seller maturity on Cursor enterprise controls?
options: Memorizing every page of the trust center verbatim.
Knowing the vocabulary, knowing where to confirm specifics, and knowing when to bring in a specialist.
Avoiding the security conversation until contract signature.
Forwarding every Security question to engineering without context.
explanation: You do not need to be a CISO. You need vocabulary, sourcing discipline, and the judgment to escalate when precision matters.

question: Which workflow attachment is the strongest opener for Cursor with a platform team?
options: ‘Want a free t-shirt?’
‘Where do migrations and refactors lose time today, and what would you measure if those got faster?’
‘Have you considered switching git providers?’
‘Do you need a new pricing model?’
explanation: Platform teams own cross-cutting work like migrations and refactors. Anchor the conversation to that workflow and a measurable outcome — that is where Cursor shows up best.
